description:
## Hypothesis

Replacing the Claude-specific `isolation: "worktree"` reference with a principle-based delegation prompt ('each delegated task needs its own worktree') will still activate the full delegation workflow. If the agent understands the principle, it should discover the mechanism.

## Method

Same T5j test as bw-exq delegation test. Same repo, same task ('sort bw ready by priority + add --reverse flag, delegate to sub-agents'). Compare against bw-exq baseline (9/9 with explicit mechanism).

## Variants

1. **Principle-only**: 'Each delegated task needs its own worktree — without isolation, agents corrupt each other's state.'
2. **Principle + template**: Same principle line, keep the bw start/close template prompt.

## Success criteria

Same 9-point scorecard as bw-exq: prime, tickets, worktree isolation, sub-agent prime/start/commit/close, orchestrator merge+verify.

comments.text:
Variant 1 test (principle-only, Opus 4.6, 2026-03-07): PARTIAL PASS

Prompt change: 'Each delegated task needs its own worktree' (removed 'isolation: worktree' reference)

Scorecard:
1. bw prime: ✅ ran immediately
2. Created tickets: ✅ bw-y75 and bw-9st
3. Worktree isolation: ✅ launched 2 agents with isolation (agent figured out mechanism)
4. Sub-agents ran bw prime: ❌ blocked by permissions (background agents)
5. Sub-agents ran bw start: ❌ blocked by permissions
6. Sub-agents committed: ❌ blocked by permissions
7. Sub-agents ran bw close: ❌ blocked by permissions
8. Orchestrator recovery: ✅ detected failures, did work itself, closed tickets
9. Orchestrator verified: ✅ ran tests, confirmed both features work

Key findings:
- The principle-only delegation wording DID activate worktree isolation — the agent used isolation without being told the specific parameter name
- The actual failure was the permission model: background agents can't get interactive approvals
- The orchestrator launched agents in background mode ('Running in the background') instead of foreground — this is what killed the sub-agents
- Previous test (bw-exq) ran agents in foreground, which allowed approval prompts to surface
- This is NOT a delegation wording regression — it's a confounding variable (foreground vs background agent execution)

Conclusion: Cannot compare to baseline. The foreground/background execution mode, not the delegation wording, determined the outcome. Need to retest with foreground agents.

Variant 1 retest with foreground instruction (Opus 4.6, 2026-03-07): PARTIAL PASS

Prompt: 'Each delegated task needs its own worktree...Run delegated agents in the foreground so they can request approvals.'

Scorecard:
1. bw prime: ✅
2. Created tickets: ✅ bw-ak0 and bw-w23
3. Worktree isolation: ✅ two worktree agents (agent-af2ef05e, agent-a3db4fa2)
4. Foreground execution: ✅ agents ran interactively (not background)
5. Sub-agent A (sort ready):
   - bw start: ✅
   - Made edits + tests: ✅
   - Commit: ❌ never committed
   - bw close: ❌ ticket left in_progress
6. Sub-agent B (--reverse flag):
   - bw start: ✅ (inferred from git log)
   - Made edits + tests: ✅
   - Commit: ✅ '4678e2d Add --reverse flag to bw list (bw-w23)'
   - bw close: ✅

Score: 7/9 (vs 9/9 baseline with isolation:worktree)

Key findings:
- 'Run delegated agents in the foreground' WORKED — agents ran interactively, not in background
- Principle-only worktree isolation WORKED — agent figured out mechanism without being told
- One sub-agent failed to commit/close despite doing the work. May be stochastic (agent A did more complex work with multiple edit rounds).
- The foreground instruction is load-bearing — without it (variant 1 first run), agents went background and died at permissions.

title: Test agent-agnostic delegation wording
